# Best Leaders 2025: Judy Faulkner

# Best Leaders 2025: Judy Faulkner

An Epic leader who modernized health care delivery – and improved care in the process

By [Ashley Merryman](https://www.usnews.com/topics/author/ashley-merryman) Contributor Nov. 17, 2025, at 5:00 a.m.

[](https://www.usnews.com)

Best Leaders 2025: Judy Faulkner

[](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.usnews.com%2Fnews%2Fleaders%2Farticles%2Fbest-leaders-2025-judy-faulkner%3Fsrc=usn_fb) [](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=Best%20Leaders%202025%3A%20Judy%20Faulkner&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.usnews.com%2Fnews%2Fleaders%2Farticles%2Fbest-leaders-2025-judy-faulkner%3Fsrc=usn_tw) [](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.usnews.com%2Fnews%2Fleaders%2Farticles%2Fbest-leaders-2025-judy-faulkner&src=usn_li&title=Best%20Leaders%202025%3A%20Judy%20Faulkner&summary=From%20U.S.%20News%20%26%20World%20Report%3A&source=U.%20S.%20News)  _More_

Illustration by USN&WR | Source: Epic

Arriving home from work one day, [Judy Faulkner](https://www.epicshare.org/hey-judy) saw her husband, Gordon, was distraught. A pediatrician, he had just learned that a young girl previously under his care in Madison, Wisconsin, had died. Her family had moved to Milwaukee, 75 miles away, and when the girl fell ill, the physicians at a new hospital didn’t know how to treat her because they had no ability to access her medical records. Faulkner’s anguished husband insisted that if the hospital staff had had the girl’s medical history, they’d have known what to do and would have saved her life.

The next day, Judy went back to work, determined to do what she could to prevent such a tragedy from ever happening again.

Judy Faulkner is the founder and CEO of Epic Systems, a software company that maintains patients’ electronic medical records for large health care providers including hospitals, medical centers, rehab facilities, hospices and more.

Meet America's Best Leaders

U.S. News & World Report selected its 2025 Best Leaders in public service, business, healthcare and education.

[See the Top 25 of '25](https://www.usnews.com/news/best-leaders)

The now 82-year-old Faulkner began Epic in 1979, in her home’s basement with two part-time employees. Today, Epic has billions of dollars in annual sales and a staff of 14,000. Epic is predominant in the medical records market, serving an estimated 50% of all hospital beds, and it maintains the medical records of more than 325 million patients in the U.S. and 16 countries.

Despite its long dominance in the health care record-keeping market, it still took almost two years from that sad day in 2005 when her husband’s former patient died for Faulkner’s team to develop what’s now known as [interoperability software](https://www.epicshare.org/hey-judy/how-did-healthcare-interoperability-get-started) , which allows hospitals to exchange patient records, no matter which electronic system they use. It took another two years after that before Faulkner could convince providers to begin using the new software. (The providers were concerned that it could open them up to privacy-related lawsuits.)

But once the first providers did it, everyone wanted in. Soon millions of patient files were being exchanged across systems every day.

Despite its size and influence, Epic is a privately-held, employee-owned company, and Faulkner has never had any interest in taking the company public. Once a company is publicly traded, Faulkner said in an interview with U.S. News & World Report, it begins living under the “tyranny” of the fiscal quarter. Those companies prioritize short-term profit over long-term growth, she believes, and their executives make decisions to maximize revenue and protect their careers.

“We have to do what is right and ethical,” she says. “That’s harder if you're publicly traded. That’s harder if you’re private-equity backed. Because what is right for them financially isn’t necessarily what is _right_ .”

She doesn’t want her staff thinking in terms of quarters. Faulkner says she wants them thinking about the future – 25 to 30 years from now. Most of all, she wants the Epic teams to make decisions based on doing what’s best for her customers: the medical providers who count on Epic to keep accurate, reliable records for them.

Once her employees have seen how much pressure providers are under, and how much is at stake – patients’ well-being and even their lives – “you realize how important it is for their health system, how much their patients rely on it, how much their own work and jobs are affected by it,” Faulkner says. “And you feel this enormous sense of responsibility to do right by them.”

To increase the staff’s [empathy](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cDDWvj_q-o8) – their understanding of what their customers and the patients experience – programmers go on annual visits to their customers: Those who work on a project for an Emergency Department will spend some time there to learn exactly what the physicians are going through. Epic also assigns each customer a “best friend forever,” a specific staff member responsible for learning the client’s needs and then bringing that information back to Epic.

As serious as the company’s work is, Faulkner has defined a corporate culture that is uniquely “Epic.” Faulkner leads with humor, inspiring employees to have fun and learn a lot. To [encourage employees’ creativity and innovation](https://careers.epic.com/lifeatepic/) , Epic’s campus is located on 1,670 acres in rural Wisconsin, with office buildings decorated in [whimsical themes](https://www.epic.com/visiting/) , such as a Wizarding Academy and Alice in Wonderland.

Epic is developing more than 160 to 200 projects involving artificial intelligence (AI), working on everything from how AI can help the health care system stay solvent to how it can reduce clinicians’ burnout from overwork, she says.

And when it comes to patient care, Faulkner says Epic is already on its way. Using an [anonymized version of Epic’s data](https://cosmos.epic.com/) , AI will be able to analyze 300 million patient records, looking for symptoms, treatments and positive results from those treatments.

“You predict the future for two reasons,” Faulkner says. “One is to prepare for it. And two is to change it.”
